Believes that taking iron supplements routinely will improve her anemia, even without being told she is iron-deficient.
Assumes that if her hemoglobin is only a little low, there is no risk of complications like gallstones or an enlarged spleen.
Worries she might be contagious because her blood cells are described as 'abnormal.'

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling really tired lately, and my eyes look a little yellow sometimes.” Volunteer without prompting: fatigue even after sleep, intermittent yellow eyes, and getting short of breath faster on stairs. If asked about urine/stool, pain, or other symptoms, mention: sometimes dark urine, mild left-sided belly discomfort/fullness, headaches when very worn out, and that similar tired spells happened before—especially after illnesses. Do not offer unless directly asked: occasional racing heartbeat when standing/climbing stairs, rare nighttime leg cramps, and that you were told your spleen was a bit big. Only if asked about sex/relationships, admit you’ve been avoiding sex due to embarrassment about looking yellow. Express misconceptions: you wondered if it’s low iron because you crave ice; you thought yellow eyes might be from something you ate or a “liver cleanse.” If the clinician shows empathy, respond with relief and openness, but bring up worries about cost of genetic testing/bloodwork and missing work for frequent visits.

Avoids recommending splenectomy as routine first-line management; if discussed, frames it as subtype-dependent and specialist-guided due to thrombotic risk in some hereditary stomatocytosis variants.
